responses: We built an AI system that generates and continuously refines treatment pathway recommendations for oncology patients at a tertiary care hospital network. The system ingests structured clinical data — pathology reports, genomic panel results, staging information, prior treatment history, comorbidity profile — and produces ranked treatment pathway options with evidence citations, contraindication checks, and clinical trial eligibility flags. The system is used by tumor board coordinators to prepare case summaries for weekly multidisciplinary team reviews. It does not make treatment decisions; it prepares the information architecture for the physicians who do. The problem it solves is case preparation time: coordinators were spending 3-5 hours per patient preparing tumor board summaries, limiting the number of cases that could be reviewed weekly. After 14 months in production, case preparation time has dropped to 45 minutes, weekly tumor board throughput has increased from 12 to 28 cases, and a retrospective audit found that AI-prepared summaries surfaced clinical trial eligibility for 31% more patients than coordinator-prepared summaries, because the system queries trial registries with precision that coordinators could not match manually. We measure the system's performance monthly against an oncologist-audited gold standard, maintaining a recall rate above 95% for tier-1 treatment options and a false positive rate below 3% for contraindication flags.

We evaluated four architectural approaches over an 18-month design period before clinical deployment. A structured knowledge graph based on clinical guidelines was considered first — it was interpretable and auditable but could not incorporate emerging evidence from recent trials and was too brittle to handle the combinatorial complexity of genomic variant combinations. A retrieval system over oncology literature was evaluated next — it surfaced relevant evidence but could not synthesize across multiple evidence sources or check a patient's specific genomic profile against the retrieved evidence without substantial manual post-processing. A supervised recommendation model trained on historical tumor board decisions was rejected explicitly because it would encode historical treatment patterns including patterns influenced by access disparities and insurance factors that we did not want to replicate. We chose a hybrid architecture: a knowledge-graph backbone for known guideline-anchored pathways, plus an LLM retrieval and synthesis layer for novel genomic combinations and emerging evidence. The explicit tradeoffs accepted: the LLM layer is not interpretable at the token level, which means we cannot explain every recommendation in mechanistic terms for regulatory or legal purposes; and the system requires quarterly clinical knowledge review to prevent the knowledge graph from diverging from current guideline standards as guidelines update.

Our risk register for this system has 9 items. I will describe the top four in detail. First, the highest-consequence failure mode: the system recommends a treatment pathway that is contraindicated for the patient's genomic profile, and this passes tumor board review because coordinators and physicians rely on the AI contraindication check rather than performing independent verification. Mitigation: contraindication flags require dual independent verification — the system flags, and a pharmacist performs a separate check against the patient record before the case reaches tumor board. The system's contraindication output is never shown to tumor board without this pharmacist sign-off, which is tracked in the workflow system. Recovery: if a contraindicated recommendation is caught post-tumor-board, the clinical case is reopened immediately and the incident is reported to our AI governance committee within 24 hours; every such incident triggers a root cause analysis and a review of the contraindication check logic. Second: clinical trial eligibility false negatives — the system fails to surface a relevant trial that the patient qualifies for. Mitigation: trial registry queries are refreshed every 48 hours; the system is tested against a curated set of 40 known-eligible patient profiles monthly to verify trial detection. Third: hallucinated evidence citations — the LLM layer generates a treatment recommendation with a citation to a study that does not exist or does not support the stated conclusion. Mitigation: all citations are validated against a verified literature index before display; citations that cannot be validated are suppressed and flagged for human review. Fourth: knowledge graph staleness causing outdated guideline recommendations when guidelines update between quarterly reviews. Mitigation: clinical guideline update alerts are subscribed to from major oncology societies; when an alert is received, a clinical review is triggered within 5 business days to assess impact on the knowledge graph.

This project has changed how I think about the relationship between AI capability and human oversight. The most important learning is that the appropriate override framework is not static — it must evolve as you accumulate evidence about where the AI is reliable and where it is not, and as the clinical context changes. In the first 6 months, we required physician review of every AI output. In months 7-12, we ran an analysis of which output categories had zero confirmed errors in the first 6 months — these were routine pathway recommendations for well-characterized tumor types with limited genomic complexity. For these categories, we moved to spot-check review rather than universal review, freeing physician time for the edge cases where AI performance was less reliable. We developed an explicit decision framework for this: AI outputs are eligible for spot-check review (rather than universal review) only when they meet three criteria simultaneously — the output falls in a category with a confirmed 0% error rate over a minimum 6-month window; the patient's clinical profile is within the distribution of training cases for that category; and no novel genomic variants are present. Outputs that fail any criterion remain under universal review. We caught the model generating a plausible-sounding chemotherapy regimen with a dosing schedule that was internally consistent but did not match any published protocol for the specific combination — it had synthesized two different protocol structures into a novel schedule that had never been validated. We caught it during our pharmacist verification step. After investigation, we found the model was interpolating between two retrieved protocols when the exact combination was not in its knowledge graph. We added an explicit check: if the LLM synthesizes a regimen that does not exactly match a protocol in our validated protocol library, the output is flagged as 'synthesized — requires clinical validation' before display.

rationale: Near-perfect L5. All dimensions at 5 except override evidence at 4. Override evidence earns 4 — a documented decision framework exists for spot-check vs universal review with clear criteria, and a specific hallucinated output was caught and root-caused. It falls short of 5 because the framework was built reactively from incidents rather than as a proactive systematic design from the outset.
